title: Cardamom enriched loaf
subtitle: A tender, subtly spiced Scandinavian-style tin loaf with butter, milk and egg
excerpt: Milk, butter and egg give this loaf a soft, close crumb and a golden crust that browns to a deep amber. Ground cardamom runs through the dough from the first mix, becoming more pronounced as the loaf rests. It is a slightly sweet bread that works well with butter and honey or alongside cheese. The process takes about 2 hours 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Cardamom-spiced enriched breads are central to Scandinavian baking, appearing in Norwegian julekake, Swedish kardemummabullar and Finnish pulla. The enriched tin loaf format here draws on the British enriched bread tradition, with the cardamom content of the Scandinavian versions. Paula Pryke documents similar spiced loaves in 'The Scandinavian Kitchen' (2010).

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze for up to 2 months. Toast directly from frozen or thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can cold-proof overnight in the tin in the fridge. Bake cold from the fridge the following morning, adding 5 minutes to the bake time.

baking.steamMethod: water tray on the oven floor for the first 10 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A bread dough that contains significant amounts of fat, sugar, eggs or dairy in addition to the standard flour, water, salt and yeast. These additions soften the gluten structure and slow fermentation, which is why enriched doughs take longer to rise than lean doughs and often use slightly more yeast. The fat coats gluten strands, producing a tender, close crumb that stays moist for longer. Enriched doughs are also denser in the tin than lean doughs and should not be expected to double in size during bulk ferment.

content.text: , which means the rise is slower and more modest than a plain white loaf. Do not expect it to double during the bulk ferment; a rise of about 50 percent is correct. Patience here pays off in a much softer, more tender crumb.

Use freshly ground cardamom if you have a spice grinder: crack the pods, discard the husks and grind the seeds. Pre-ground cardamom works but loses potency quickly. The flavour should be clearly present in the finished loaf without being dominant.

Combine the 400 g flour, 6 g yeast, 7 g salt, 2 tsp ground cardamom and 30 g caster sugar. Add the 200 ml tepid milk, 40 g softened butter and 1 beaten egg. Mix to a dough, then knead for 10 minutes until smooth and only slightly tacky. The butter should be fully incorporated with no greasy streaks remaining.
Place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover and leave at room temperature for 70 to 80 minutes until the dough has risen by about half. It will not double; this is correct for an enriched dough.

Grease a 900 g loaf tin. Knock back the dough gently and shape into a cylinder. Place seam-side down in the tin. Brush with the beaten egg wash, cover loosely and proof for 40 to 50 minutes until the dough has risen above the rim and springs back slowly when pressed.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan with a small water tray on the bottom shelf. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until the top is deep amber brown, the sides have pulled from the tin, and the base sounds hollow when tapped. An internal probe should read 93 to 95 degrees C. Cool on a wire rack for at least 20 minutes before slicing.

items.problem: Loaf barely rose during the bulk ferment
items.solution: Enriched doughs rise more slowly than lean doughs. If the kitchen is cool, the dough may need up to 2 hours. The rise should be about 50 percent, not a doubling. If it has not moved at all after 2 hours, the yeast may have been killed by hot milk or butter; both must be at or below 35 degrees C.

items.problem: Crust browns too quickly before the loaf is baked through
items.solution: The egg wash accelerates browning. Lay a sheet of foil loosely over the top after 15 minutes and continue baking. The internal probe reading is the reliable test for doneness.

items.problem: Dense, close crumb with no spring
items.solution: The dough was over-kneaded or under-proofed. Enriched doughs need enough proof time to develop a light structure before baking. Wait for the dough to rise above the rim before loading into the oven.
